mnemonics.title: Sequential vs Direct Access
mnemonics.content: SAM REWIND = Sequential Access Method requires reading everything when you want a specific record. DIRECT IS BETTER = Direct access allows jumping to any record instantly.

mnemonics.title: ISAM Components
mnemonics.content: ISAM = INDEX + SEQUENTIAL + ACCESS METHOD. Think of it as a BOOK (index = table of contents, sequential = pages in order, access = finding any chapter quickly).

mnemonics.title: Access Method Selection
mnemonics.content: SQUID = Sequential for Queries (sequential), Unique keys use Direct access, Indexed for both.

mnemonics.title: Relative Address Formula
mnemonics.content: RRN MINUS ONE TIMES SIZE = (RRN - 1) × RecordSize gives the byte offset from start.

acronyms.usage: Reading records one after another; used in magnetic tapes and sequential file processing
Accessing any record directly by address; used in disk-based storage systems
File organization combining sequential storage with index for fast lookup; used in databases
IBM's access method providing both sequential and direct access using B-tree index; used in mainframes
The sequential number assigned to each record in a file for direct access calculation
Self-balancing tree data structure maintaining sorted data and allowing logarithmic search operations

visualTips.visualization: Think of a cassette tape - you must fast-forward or rewind through all songs to reach a specific one. The tape head reads everything in order.
Think of a DVD with chapters - you can directly jump to chapter 10 using the remote without watching chapters 1-9. The laser instantly positions to any location.
Think of a book table of contents - you look up the topic in the index (fast), find the page number, and directly flip to that page (efficient).
Imagine a library catalog system - the card catalog (index) tells you exactly where the book (record) is shelved, combining quick lookup with physical storage order.
Think of a waiter refilling water glasses - they collect multiple empty glasses and fill them all at once (batch I/O) instead of making separate trips for each glass.

analogies.analogy: Like reading a book from page 1 to find a specific sentence - you must read through all previous pages. Perfect for reading the entire book (batch processing).
Like using page numbers in a dictionary - you directly flip to the 'M' section to find 'mouse' without checking every page. Perfect for quick lookups.
Like a library's index card system - the cards (index) tell you exactly where each book is located, combining the speed of direct access with the organization of sequential storage.
Like a delivery truck carrying multiple packages in one trip instead of making separate trips for each package - reduces travel (I/O) time significantly.
Like your finger marking your place in a physical book - it remembers where you stopped reading so you can continue from exactly that spot.
